lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: ’Tis certain he hath pass’d the river Somme.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: And if he be not fought withal, my lord,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Let us not live in France; let us quit all

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: And give our vineyards to a barbarous people.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: O Dieu vivant! shall a few sprays of us,

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: The emptying of our fathers’ luxury,

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Our scions, put in wild and savage stock,

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Spirt up so suddenly into the clouds,

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: And overlook their grafters?

lines.speaker: BOURBON
lines.text: Normans, but bastard Normans, Norman bastards!

lines.speaker: BOURBON
lines.text: Mort de ma vie! if they march along

lines.speaker: BOURBON
lines.text: Unfought withal, but I will sell my dukedom,

lines.speaker: BOURBON
lines.text: To buy a slobbery and a dirty farm

lines.speaker: BOURBON
lines.text: In that nook-shotten isle of Albion.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Dieu de batailles! where have they this mettle?

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Is not their climate foggy, raw, and dull,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: On whom, as in despite, the sun looks pale,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Killing their fruit with frowns? Can sodden water,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: A drench for sur-rein’d jades, their barley-broth,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Decoct their cold blood to such valiant heat?

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: And shall our quick blood, spirited with wine,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Seem frosty? O, for honour of our land,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Let us not hang like roping icicles

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Upon our houses’ thatch, whiles a more frosty people

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Sweat drops of gallant youth in our rich fields!

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Poor we may call them in their native lords.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: By faith and honour,

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Our madams mock at us, and plainly say

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Our mettle is bred out and they will give

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Their bodies to the lust of English youth

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: To new-store France with bastard warriors.

lines.speaker: BOURBON
lines.text: They bid us to the English dancing-schools,

lines.speaker: BOURBON
lines.text: And teach lavoltas high and swift corantos;

lines.speaker: BOURBON
lines.text: Saying our grace is only in our heels,

lines.speaker: BOURBON
lines.text: And that we are most lofty runaways.

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Where is Montjoy the herald? speed him hence:

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Let him greet England with our sharp defiance.

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Up, princes! and, with spirit of honour edged

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: More sharper than your swords, hie to the field:

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Charles Delabreth, high constable of France;

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: You Dukes of Orleans, Bourbon, and of Berri,

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Alençon, Brabant, Bar, and Burgundy;

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Jacques Chatillon, Rambures, Vaudemont,

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Beaumont, Grandpré, Roussi, and Fauconbridge,

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Foix, Lestrale, Bouciqualt, and Charolois;

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: High dukes, great princes, barons, lords and knights,

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: For your great seats now quit you of great shames.

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Bar Harry England, that sweeps through our land

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: With pennons painted in the blood of Harfleur:

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Rush on his host, as doth the melted snow

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Upon the valleys, whose low vassal seat

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: The Alps doth spit and void his rheum upon:

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Go down upon him, you have power enough,

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: And in a captive chariot into Rouen

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Bring him our prisoner.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: This becomes the great.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Sorry am I his numbers are so few,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: His soldiers sick and famish’d in their march,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: For I am sure, when he shall see our army,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: He’ll drop his heart into the sink of fear

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: And for achievement offer us his ransom.

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Therefore, lord constable, haste on Montjoy,

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: And let him say to England that we send

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: To know what willing ransom he will give.

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Prince Dauphin, you shall stay with us in Rouen.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Not so, I do beseech your majesty.

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Be patient, for you shall remain with us.

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Now forth, lord constable and princes all,

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: And quickly bring us word of England’s fall.

stage_directions.text: Enter the King of France, the Dauphin, the Duke of Bourbon, the Constable of France, and others.

glosses.definition: the river Henry had to cross before turning north to Calais — historically a difficult crossing
given battle against
barbaric people — the Constable's view of the English
French: 'O living God' — exclamation of aristocratic dismay
offshoots of us — the French see the English as scions of their Norman stock
the dregs of our ancestors' lust — Dauphin's gross image
young shoots grafted onto another stock
English soil as uncultivated root-stock — French Normans grafted onto it
sprout up; shoot up
stand taller than those who grafted them — the English outdo the Norman ancestors

glosses.phrase: Normans, but bastard Normans, Norman bastards
glosses.definition: Bourbon's insult — Anglo-Normans as illegitimate descendants
French: 'Death of my life' — strong oath
a muddy, filthy farm
angular, crook-cornered island — 'Albion' is the classical name for Britain; 'nook-shotten' = having many corners or bays
French: 'God of battles'
spirit; temperament — metallurgical metaphor
damp, cold, and sluggish — stereotype of English climate
boiled water — for making beer
medicinal drink for over-worked horses — the Constable's contempt for English ale
beer — the thin barley beer of the English
boil up their cold blood into heat — cooking metaphor for the English paradox
animated by wine — French aristocratic drink versus English beer
icicles hanging like ropes

glosses.phrase: Poor we may call them in their native lords
glosses.definition: we may call the English poor in their own nobility — i.e., lacking the French refinement
Frenchwomen — 'our ladies'
our valour has died out by over-breeding
repopulate France with the illegitimate children of English soldiers — Dauphin's humiliation fantasy
Dauphin reduces French war to training in English dance-halls
the lavolta — a fashionable 16th-century dance involving a high leap
the coranto — a quick court dance with running steps

glosses.phrase: grace is only in our heels
glosses.definition: our grace lies only in running away
high-class fugitives — self-flattering cowardice
the French royal herald — titular name, not personal; he will be the French emissary to Henry
Charles d'Albret — the historical Constable of France, killed at Agincourt
French nobles named in the historical record of Agincourt

glosses.phrase: pennons painted in the blood of Harfleur
glosses.definition: banners dyed red with Harfleur's blood
spit his phlegm — the Alps spitting on the valleys below
a chariot transporting a prisoner — the French King imagines Henry led in triumph
capital of Normandy — a French seat of power
the pit of his fear — 'sink' = drain, cesspool

glosses.phrase: for achievement offer us his ransom
glosses.definition: in exchange for safe-passage, offer his ransom money — the French expect Henry to buy his way out

annotations.title: The French self-diagnosis — Norman bastards and cold blood
annotations.body: The Dauphin and Bourbon's speech about the English is one of the most politically revealing moments in the play. Their complaint is genealogical: the English are 'Normans, but bastard Normans, Norman bastards,' i.e. the French admit that the English invaders are descendants of their own Norman ancestors (via 1066), degraded by mixing with the 'wild and savage stock' of England. The Constable's speech that follows elaborates this into a climatic argument: how can a foggy, raw, cold country, living on beer ('sur-rein'd jades' drench), produce warriors hot enough to fight? The passage is doing work on two levels. Internally, it is French panic expressed as ethnic self-regard. Externally, Shakespeare is flattering his English audience: the French themselves admit that English valour is a mystery. The anxiety about mass rape ('our mettle is bred out, and they will give / Their bodies to the lust of English youth / To new-store France with bastard warriors') is especially striking: it inverts and acknowledges the Harfleur threat. The French fear what Henry just threatened.
sources: Andrew Gurr (ed.), King Henry V (New Cambridge Shakespeare, 1992)
Willy Maley, Nation, State, and Empire in English Renaissance Literature (2003)

trials.prompt: What does the Constable claim distinguishes the English at line 15 onwards?
options: That their cold, foggy climate and beer-fed diet should disqualify them as warriors — yet they fight hot and bravely, a paradox Constable finds shameful for France

trials.prompt: What does the Dauphin fear at lines 27–30?
options: That French women will give themselves to English soldiers and repopulate France with 'bastard warriors' — mass rape as ethnic replacement, the very threat Henry made at Harfleur
That the Dauphin will be captured
That the English army lacks bread
